trials.justification: All 6 required rules identified (BR-1 through BR-6, plus bonus BR-7). Regulatory rules (license verification BR-1, record visibility BR-5, note lock immutability BR-7) correctly marked invariant: true. BR-6 (48h window) marked invariant: false with nuanced justification distinguishing the time threshold from the locking concept itself — defensible. Role-based cancellation correctly split into BR-3 (patient, time-bound) and BR-4 (admin, unrestricted). 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria far exceed the minimum of 4, covering all required scenarios: licensed physician booking (AC-1, AC-2), double-booking attempt (AC-3, AC-4), late cancellation attempt (AC-5, AC-6, AC-7), and unauthorized record access (AC-8, AC-9). observations.text: Exceptional ubiquitous language section with 12 precisely defined terms — well beyond what was required
Correctly split cancellation into two rules (BR-3/BR-4) capturing the patient vs admin role boundary explicitly
Added BR-7 (note immutability after locking) as a separate invariant from the 48h window — a sophisticated distinction between the time policy and the state transition integrity
12 acceptance criteria with concrete time values (e.g., 12:30 for 15:00 appointment) make scenarios unambiguous and directly testable
Edge cases identify real operational gaps: DST handling, concurrent booking races, license revocation post-booking, cancelled appointment notes
Escalations provide structured options with recommendations — actionable for product/compliance stakeholders
Confidence assumptions are specific and testable (adjacent appointments not overlapping, boundary inclusivity, UTC convention)

BR-6 (48h note locking) classified as invariant: false is debatable — the rubric expects record lock marked invariant: true. The justification is reasonable (the 48h duration is configurable) but the concept of locking for clinical integrity is regulatory. Bird partially compensated by making BR-7 (immutability once locked) invariant: true, effectively splitting the concern.
